coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-induced robust ε-MnO2 electrode by synergistic engineering of oxygen vacancy and low crystallinity for high-performance flexible asymmetric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Quan, Hongying
Zeng, Weiliang
Chen, Wenchao
Wang, Yan
Tao, Wenhao
Chen, Dezhi
coredata.dc:description: Developing high-capacitance and robust electrode materials for robust flexible supercapacitors is an ongoing challenge. To fabricate such energy storage devices, flexible electrodes with desired electrochemical and mechanical performances are crucial. Composition and structure tailoring of electrode materials play a significant role in exploiting high-performance flexible supercapacitors. Herein, CQDs-modified ε-MnO2 nanosheets with abundant oxygen vacancies and low crystallinity was uniformly grown on the skeletons of carbon cloth by a facile one-step electrodeposition strategy. Benefiting from the structural features and intrinsic defects, the obtained CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode delivered outstanding mechanical property with tensile strength of 87 MPa and excellent electrochemical activity with high specific capacitance of 334.5 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate capability as well as good cycling stability with 90% of capacitance retention after 6000 cycles at 5 A g−1, which was much better than pristine ε-MnO2 electrode. In addition, the assembled aqueous asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) with good flexibility based on the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode could provide an energy density of 68.2 Wh kg−1 with power density of 990 W kg−1 at 5 A g−1 and long-term stability, outperforming most currently available flexible supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flexible supercapacitors
ε-MnO2
Carbon quantum dot
Carbon cloth
Oxygen vacancy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrodeposition of CQDs/ε-MnO2 on carbon cloth

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the fabrication of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrode.

Characterization of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes. a) XRD patterns, b) Raman spectra, c) FTIR spectrum, d) full XPS spectra, XPS spectra of e) Mn 3 s, f) Mn 2p, g) O1s, h) EPR spectra, and e) UV-DRS spectra.
SEM images. a-b) pristine ε-MnO2 on CC, c-d) CQDs/ε-MnO2 on CC, e) pristine ε-MnO2 on CC, and f) CQDs/ε-MnO2 on CC after tensile test, g) stress-strain curves of the obtained samples.
a) TEM, b-c) HRTEM, d) STEM, and e-f) corresponding EDS mapping images of ε-MnO2; g) TEM, h-j) HRTEM, k) STEM, and l-n) corresponding EDS mapping images of CQDs/ε-MnO2.

Electrochemical performances of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrodes. a) CV curves at 50 mV s−1, b) GCD curves at 1 A g−1; c-) CV curves of CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode at various scan rates; d) GCD curves of CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode at various current densities; e) calculated specific capacitances versus current densities, f) Nyquist plots, g) long-term cycling stability at 5 A g−1; h) CV curves of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode at the different bending states; i) photograph of the bending test.

Electrochemical performances of CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC. a) CV curves tested at varied voltage windows at 50 mV s−1; b) GCD curves tested at varied voltage windows at 1 A g−1; c) CV curves at various scan rates; d) GCD curves at various current densities; e) specific capacitance versus current densities; f) Ragone plots and comparison with previously reported literature; g) cycling stability at 5 A g−1; commercial electronic compass powered by CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC in h) normal and i) bent state.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-induced robust ε-MnO2 electrode by synergistic engineering of oxygen vacancy and low crystallinity for high-performance flexible asymmetric supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National-Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Heavy Metal Pollutant Control and Resource utilization, Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National-Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Heavy Metal Pollutant Control and Resource utilization, Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National-Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Heavy Metal Pollutant Control and Resource utilization, Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing high-capacitance and robust electrode materials for robust flexible supercapacitors is an ongoing challenge. To fabricate such energy storage devices, flexible electrodes with desired electrochemical and mechanical performances are crucial. Composition and structure tailoring of electrode materials play a significant role in exploiting high-performance flexible supercapacitors. Herein, CQDs-modified ε-MnO2 nanosheets with abundant oxygen vacancies and low crystallinity was uniformly grown on the skeletons of carbon cloth by a facile one-step electrodeposition strategy. Benefiting from the structural features and intrinsic defects, the obtained CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode delivered outstanding mechanical property with tensile strength of 87 MPa and excellent electrochemical activity with high specific capacitance of 334.5 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate capability as well as good cycling stability with 90% of capacitance retention after 6000 cycles at 5 A g−1, which was much better than pristine ε-MnO2 electrode. In addition, the assembled aqueous asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) with good flexibility based on the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode could provide an energy density of 68.2 Wh kg−1 with power density of 990 W kg−1 at 5 A g−1 and long-term stability, outperforming most currently available flexible supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of flexible wearable electronics in the commercial market, there has been an increasing demand for high-performance and multifunctional characteristics in the field of flexible energy storage devices [1–4]. Among the diverse flexible energy storage devices, flexible asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) have aroused extensive concerns due to their broader voltage window compared to symmetric supercapacitors [5–7]. ASCs usually composed of two electrode materials with distinct charge storage mechanisms, e.g., a pseudocapacitive electrode and a double-layer capacitive electrode with complementary voltage windows [8–10]. Therefore, exploiting appropriate electrode becomes the prerequisite for designing flexible asymmetric supercapacitor with desirable electrochemical performance.

Manganese dioxide (MnO2) with various polymorphs is intensively explored as the positive electrode materials for ASCs on account of their various crystal structures, variable Mn oxidation states, outstanding theoretical specific capacitance (1370 F g−1), and broad potential windows ∼1 V in the aqueous electrolyte [11]. Recently, ε-MnO2, which derives from the ramsdellite structure, spring up as a promising catalyst for rechargeable batteries [12–15]. Like γ-MnO2, the tunnel-like structure of ε-MnO2 can serve as energy storage site, which renders it a decent candidate for electrodes of supercapacitors. However, the capacitive performance of ε-MnO2 remained rarely explored, limited existing studies show that the capacitive performance of ε-MnO2-based supercapacitor is not very excellent because of the low electron transfer efficiency and underutilized electrochemically active sites, which impede their potential application in high-performance supercapacitors [16–20]. Furthermore, the energy density of the most assembled flexible ASCs based on the ε-MnO2 electrode is far below expected, leading to poor application satisfaction because of the low practical capacitance and frustrating structural collapse during the repetitive charging/discharging process [18].

Strategies such as doping with heteroatoms [21], modifying with highly conductive compound [22], and inducing oxygen vacancies [23] have been investigated to create more electrochemically active sites for better capacitive performance and to ensure the structural integrity of MnO2-based electrodes. Among these methods, introducing of oxygen vacancies has attracted broad attention to achieve the favorable electrochemical performance of MnO2 [24]. These oxygen vacancies can adjust the electronic configuration and accelerate the charge transfer, consequently optimizing the electrochemical performance of the MnO2 [25]. Besides, recent studies on amorphous or low-crystalline transition metal oxides/hydroxides have verified that low crystalline transition metal oxide/hydroxides deliver better capacitive performances than their high crystalline counterpart because of the abundant structural defects and disorder [26–29]. The low-crystalline structure of metal oxides with intrinsically isotropic behavior can effectively accommodate the strain caused by volume changes and thus improve the structural collapse of the electrode material during long-term cycling [30]. Up to now, many approaches, such as thermal treatment, chemical reduction, N2-plasma treatment, etc., have been used to introduce oxygen vacancies or to fabricate low-crystalline MnO2 [24,31,32]. However, the conditions of these methods are relatively complicated to control. Therefore, there is still a challenging to design a simple way to prepare oxygen-defective MnO2 with low-crystallinity.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained increasing attention as electroactive electrode materials because of their unique physicochemical properties [33,34]. Previous literature reports have confirmed that CQDs can improve the electrical conductivity of electrode and supply abundant active sites for charge transport during charge-discharge processes, resulting in the enhancement of the electrochemical performance of electrode [35,36], in which CQDs can serve as a structure-directing agent for the assembly of functional micro/nanomaterials due to the quantum confinement and edge effect [37–39]. However, there are very few reports about CQDs being utilized as an inducer to induce low crystallinity in electrode material.

Herein, CQDs were in-situ immobilized into ε-MnO2 by one-step electrodeposition to enhance the capacitive performance while holding the structural integrity of the ε-MnO2 electrode. Owing to their unique physicochemical properties, CQDs can play a dual role as structural stabilizer and inducer, which can effectively increase oxygen vacancies and induce low crystallinity in ε-MnO2. On the one hand, CQDs acted as structural stabilizer to stabilize the original structure of ε-MnO2 through the formation of strong interfacial bonding between ε-MnO2 and carbon cloth by the abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs, and finally obtain the robust electrodes with good mechanical properties. On the other hand, CQDs can induce low-crystalline ε-MnO2, the presence of structural disorder can significantly increase efficient active sites and promote active site exposure for energy storage. The obtained CQDs/ε-MnO2 composites were uniformly grown on the skeletons of carbon cloth, and the corresponding electrode was designated as CQDs/ε-MnO2. This structure maintains structure continuity of the ε-MnO2, enables the maximum utilization of electrochemically active sites and sufficient electron transfer efficiency by providing direct contact between CQDs/ε-MnO2 and carbon cloth, which is essential for excellent electrochemical performance. The as-prepared CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode yields improved specific capacitance of 334.5 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, while maintaining remarkable rate capability and long cycling stability (with 90% of capacitance retention after 6000 cycles), which is superior to that of pristine ε-MnO2. The characteristics of low-crystalline and rich oxygen vacancies of CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode ensure adequate pseudocapacitive reaction active sites. The structural stabilizing effect of CQDs not only stabilizes the original structure of CQDs/ε-MnO2, but also buffers the large volume variation of ε-MnO2 during the repetitive charging/discharging process. Moreover, a flexible asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) based on the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode shows an energy density up to 68.2 Wh kg−1 and outstanding cycle stability with 96.5% retention after 6 000 cycles. These results demonstrate an instructive route to achieving oxygen defect and low crystallinity for high-performance advanced electrode materials for flexible energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of CQDs/ε-MnO2 on carbon cloth
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method according to our previous works [40]. The electrodeposition CQDs/ε-MnO2 on carbon cloth was performed using a three-electrode system in a plating solution containing 0.1 M manganese acetate, 0.1 M anhydrous sodium sulfate, and 0.5 g CQDs, where a platinum foil, Ag/AgCl electrode, and carbon cloth (1 ×1.2 cm2) were used as the counter electrode, reference electrode, and work electrode, respectively. The electrochemical deposition was performed at 1 V for 200 s at room temperature. After electrodeposition, the obtained CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode was repeatedly rinsed with deionized water and dried overnight at 60 ℃. The mass loading of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 on carbon cloth was about 1.4 mg cm−2. The pure ε-MnO2 was electrodeposited on carbon cloth under the same conditions without CQDs.

The physiochemical properties of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 on carbon cloth were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Thermo Nicolet), Raman spectra (LabRAM HR800), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra X, KRATOS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 250, FEI), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X, FEI) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR, Bruker EMX PLUS), and UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI PHI5000 VersaProbe III), respectively.

The electrochemical performances of the flexible CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Electrochemical tests were performed in a three-electrode system on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai), where a platinum plate (1 cm × 1 cm), Ag/AgCl electrode, flexible CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode, and 1 M Na2SO4 were used as counter electrode, reference electrode, working electrode, and electrolyte, respectively.

The specific capacitance (Cm, F g−1) of the flexible CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode was calculated based on the Eq. (1) [41].
(1)
C
m
=
I × ∆ t
m × ∆ V
Where I (mA), Δt (s), ΔV (V), and m (g) are the applied current, the discharge time, the operation potential window, and the mass loading of the CQDs/ε-MnO2, respectively.

A flexible asymmetry supercapacitor device (ASC) was fabricated to further evaluate the practical application of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode. The CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode (positive electrode), filter paper (the separator), and activated carbon cloth (negative electrode, ACC, supporting information Section 1) were assembled in a sandwich structure, and 1 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. The assembled device was marked as CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC. The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) of CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC were calculated using the following Eqs. (2) and (3) [42], respectively.
(2)
E =
1
2 × 3.6
C
m
( Δ V )
2
(3)
P =
E × 3600
∆ t
where C m (F g−1), Δt (s), and ΔV (V) are the specific capacitance, the discharge time, and the voltage window of the CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC, respectively.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic for the electrodeposition CQDs/ε-MnO2 on the surface of carbon cloth under an applied electric field. Fig. 2a displays the XRD patterns of the obtained samples. A broad peak at 25° was assigned to the carbon cloth. The diffraction peaks at 2θ= 37.1, 42.4, 56.0, and 66.8° in the XRD pattern of pristine ε-MnO2 are consistent with crystal planes of ε-MnO2 (JCPDS, PDF No. 30–0820) [14]. The intensity of all these peaks became weaker in CQDs/ε-MnO2, indicating the appearance of lattice defects, e.g., oxygen vacancies stemmed from the in-situ embedding of CQDs, which further weakened the crystallinity of pristine ε-MnO2 and result in low crystallinity CQDs/ε-MnO2. To substantiate the above result, the Raman spectra and FTIR analysis were performed. As shown in Fig. 2b, three characteristic peaks existed in the Raman spectra of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 in the range of 500–1800 cm−1. The peak at 650 cm−1 in pristine ε-MnO2 is ascribed to the symmetric vibration of the Mn-O stretching mode in [MnO6] octahedra [43], which is sensitive to crystalline structure and oxygen vacancy defects [44,45]. The intensity of this peak in CQDs/ε-MnO2 was dramatically decreased and shifted to 630 cm−1. The obvious blue shift and reduction in peak intensity illustrate the weak Mn-O stretching vibration mode in CQDs/ε-MnO2, which should be attributed to the abundant oxygen vacancy defects and disorder within the low crystalline phase induced by the in-situ embedding of CQDs [46]. The peaks centered at about 1330 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 belong to the carbon cloth. The FTIR spectrum of pristine ε-MnO2 in Fig. 2c exhibits characteristic peaks between 380 and 470 cm−1, which are assigned to stretching and bending vibrations of the Mn-O bond, respectively [47]. The CQDs/ε-MnO2 exhibited much weaker signals for Mn-O due to the low crystallinity caused by the in-situ embedding of CQDs. The chemical states and the composition of the pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes were determined by XPS and demonstrated in Fig. 2d. The Mn, O, and C elements can be detected in both samples, and the N element in CQDs/ε-MnO2 comes from the CQDs. Fig. 2e demonstrates the Mn 3 s XPS spectra containing two spin orbit peaks. The binding energy separation (ΔE) of the two peaks can be employed to analyze the Mn average oxidation state (AOS) of the samples based on the equation: AOS= 8.956–1.126ΔE [27]. The ΔE of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 was 5.03 and 5.61 eV, and the corresponding average Mn valence of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 are 3.29 and 2.64, respectively. The lower Mn valence of CQDs/ε-MnO2 suggests that the in-situ introduction of CQDs can adjust the valence state of Mn, which may on account of that the reduction Mn4+ to Mn3+ can compensate for oxygen vacancies [48]. The Mn 2p XPS spectrum of the pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 have two characteristic peaks of 642.0 and 653.5 eV, and the spin separation energy is 11.5 eV, which can be vested in Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 of Mn4+ in MnO2 [49]. The Mn 2p3/2 XPS spectra (Fig. 2f) were used to quantify the concentration of Mn3+ and Mn4+ in the surface of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 [50]. The peak deconvolution of Mn 2p3/2 is composed of Mn3+ at 641.3 eV and Mn4+ at 642.6 eV and a satellite peak at 645.0 eV, respectively [51]. We can see that the ratio of Mn3+/Mn4+ increased from 0.33 for the bare ε-MnO2 to 0.86 for the CQDs/ε-MnO2, indicating the conversion of Mn4+ into Mn3+ after the in-situ embedding of CQDs. The high ratio of Mn3+/Mn4+ was reported in favor of accelerating the charge transfer and ions diffusion of MnO2, sequentially enhancing the conductivity of the electrode [52]. Simultaneously, the deconvoluted three peaks of Mn 2p1/2 orbital at 652.9, 654.1 and 657.5 eV were also assigned to Mn3+, Mn4+ and a satellite peak, respectively. In addition, the high ratio of Mn3+/Mn4+ suggests the presence of rich oxygen vacancies in the as-prepared CQDs/ε-MnO2, demonstrated by the high-resolution O 1 s spectra in Fig. 2g. The O 1 s spectrum of CQDs/ε-MnO2 can be identified into three peaks centered at 529.8, 531.4 and 531.6 eV, which are designated to the lattice oxygen related to tetravalent O-Mn-O, Mn-OH bond and CO/C-O, respectively [32,53]. The appearance of the CO/C-O bond in CQDs/ε-MnO2 confirmed that CQDs in-situ embed and distributed in the framework of ε-MnO2. The EPR measurement was carried out to notarize the enhancement of oxygen vacancies in CQDs/ε-MnO2 and was presented in Fig. 2h. The strong signal located at g= 2.002 gives evidence of oxygen vacancies, and the much greater peak intensity of CQDs/ε-MnO2 suggests that in-situ embedding of CQDs induced additional oxygen vacancies into the ε-MnO2. It is reported that the oxygen vacancies can regulate the electronic structure of metal oxides [54]. Therefore, the electronic structure of the pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 was examined by using UV-DRS spectra, and the optical bandgap energy was estimated by deriving the Tauc plot from the UV curves, the results are displayed in Fig. S1 and Fig. 2i. It can be seen from Fig. 2i that the CQDs/ε-MnO2 presents a narrower bandgap (2.31 eV) than pristine ε-MnO2 (2.73 eV). As we know, narrower bandgap facilitates the migration of charge carriers inside the metal oxides and resulting in higher conductivity.

To confirm that CQDs can be served as structure-directing agents to regulate the morphology of products, the morphologies of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 on carbon cloth were examined via SEM ( Fig. 3). It is observed that a layer of ε-MnO2 film uniformly wrapped on the surface of the carbon fibers through a one-step electrodeposition strategy (Fig. 3a). The amplifying SEM image in Fig. 3b shows that the pristine ε-MnO2 film was comprised of ultrathin ε-MnO2 nanosheets and these nanosheets are interweaved stacked to form a loose structure. It is found that the thickness of the coated ε-MnO2 film was about ∼1 µm (Fig. S2a), and there is an obvious interface between ε-MnO2 film and carbon fiber Fig. S2b, indicating low bonding strength and inferior mechanical property. In addition, apparent cracks can be observed in the pristine ε-MnO2 film in Fig. 3a. After the in-situ embedding of CQDs, the coated CQDs/ε-MnO2 film became dense, and no cracks existed on the surface of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 film (Fig. 3c-d). The apparent interface between the ε-MnO2 film and carbon fiber in the pristine ε-MnO2 film was disappeared in the CQDs/ε-MnO2 (Fig. S2b), and the CQDs/ε-MnO2 film tightly wrapped on the carbon fibers to form an integrated structure, suggesting good bonding strength and superior mechanical property of CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode. The BET specific surface area.

and porous nature of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 were investigated by nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements and presented in Fig. S3. The BET specific surface area of the pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 is 38.2 and 4.9 m2 g−1( Table S1). The smaller specific surface area further indicates that the more compact structure of CQDs/ε-MnO2 than pristine ε-MnO2. To verify the good mechanical properties of the prepared CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode, mechanical properties were characterized by the tensile test (Section 2, supporting information). As shown in Fig. 3e, the coated pristine ε-MnO2 film fractured under the action of tensile stress and exfoliated from the surface of carbon fiber due to the poor bonding strength between ε-MnO2 film and carbon fiber. For the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode, the coated CQDs/ε-MnO2 film fractured with carbon fiber simultaneously, and the fracture surface became coarser (Fig. 3f) because the strong interfacial bonding between CQDs/ε-MnO2 film and carbon fiber can resist more damage. The CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode exhibited a high tensile strength of 87 MPa (Fig. 3g), which is larger than that of pristine ε-MnO2 (48 MPa). The above results demonstrated that the in-situ embedding of CQDs can not only adjust the structure of the pristine ε-MnO2, but also facilitate the formation of strong interfacial bonding by the abundant functional groups on its surface, and accordingly enhance the mechanical property of the electrode.

TEM was conducted to further investigate the microstructure of the pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2, as presented in Fig. 4. The ultrathin pristine ε-MnO2 nanosheets are interconnected and stacked to form a loose structure with nanopores (Fig. 4a). The transparent character verifies the ultrathin nature of the ε-MnO2 nanosheets. The resolved lattice fringes with the spacings of 0.24 nm in HRTEM images (Fig. 4b-c) of the labeled area in Fig. 4a can be assigned to the (100) plane of ε-MnO2 (JCPDS#30–0820). Furthermore, the diffraction rings in the inset of Fig. 4b indicate the polycrystalline nature of the pristine ε-MnO2. The EDS analysis shows that the Mn and O elements are uniformly distributed across the pristine ε-MnO2 (Fig. 4d-f). By contrast, as shown in Fig. 4g, CQDs/ε-MnO2 nanosheets are interconnected to form a hierarchical structure, indicating that the in-situ embedding of CQDs could tune the interlacement of ε-MnO2 nanosheets, which will improve the structural stability of the electrode. Fig. 4h shows the HRTEM image of CQDs/ε-MnO2 of the labeled area in Fig. 4g. It is easy to see that tiny nanocrystalline CQDs are evenly embedded into the ε-MnO2 nanosheets and successfully form a composite. The HRTEM image (Fig. 4h) of the labeled area in Fig. 4g further revealed the CQDs/ε-MnO2 composite was fabricated. The lattice fringes distance of 0.24 nm in Fig. 4i matches well with the (100) plane of the ε-MnO2. Another clearly defined lattice spacings of 0.21 nm in Fig. 4j correspond to the (101) crystallographic planes of graphitic carbon. These results confirm the formation of a composite structure in the CQDs/ε-MnO2, and the excellent anchor-hold between CQDs and ε-MnO2 facilitates the exposure of electroactive sites. Furthermore, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CQDs/ε-MnO2 presented inset Fig. 4h indicated the low crystalline property after the in-situ embedding of CQDs, which agrees with the XRD results. The scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images coupled with EDS mapping (Fig. 4k-n) reveals that Mn, O, and C elements are homogeneously distributed within the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode. The mass percentage of CQDs in CQDs/ε-MnO2 was estimated through a thermogravimetry test (Fig. S4), and the result shows that the content of CQDs in CQDs/ε-MnO2 is about 0.3%.

The capacitive performances of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrodes were measured in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. To explore the influence of the amount of CQDs on the capacitive performance of CQDs/ε-MnO2, the electrochemical performance of CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes with different CQDs amount were investigated and exhibited in Fig. S5a-b. It can be concluded from these plots that the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode with CQDs content of 0.5 g presents maximum specific capacitance. The subsequent research of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 is mainly based on the electrode with CQDs content of 0.5 g. Fig. 5a illustrates the CV curves of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrodes at 10 mV s−1. The CV curves display a nearly rectangular shape, suggesting the synergistic contribution of both double-layer and pseudocapacitance behavior. Compared with pristine ε-MnO2 the CQDs/ε-MnO2 exhibited a higher current response, indicating that the in-situ embedding of CQDs could increase the specific capacitance of ε-MnO2. The same conclusion was obtained by the GCD curves (Fig. 5b) of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrodes at 1 A g−1. The nearly ideal triquetrous GCD profile of the electrodes was in accord with the rectangular shape of CV results. The quasi-symmetrical shape of GCD curves reveals the well reversible redox process and high coulombic efficiency of the electrodes. In addition, the significantly increased discharge time suggests the higher specific capacitance of CQDs/ε-MnO2 than pristine ε-MnO2, which further confirmed the improved charge-storage capability after the in-situ introduction of CQDs. Furthermore, the straight CV and GCD curves of the carbon cloth indicate that the capacitance contribution from carbon cloth was primarily negligible. The capacitive properties of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrode were investigated comprehensively, the CV and GCD curves measured at various scan rates and current densities are presented in Fig. 5c-d, respectively. The corresponding CV and GCD curves of pristine ε-MnO2 are shown in Fig. S5c-d. The CV and GCD curves of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrode still retained their original shapes even at 100 mV s−1and 10 A g−1, indicating excellent rate capability. Fig. 5e exhibits the specific capacitances calculated according to the discharge times in Fig. 5d. The specific capacitance of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode obtained was 334.5 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, surpassing the pristine ε-MnO2 (194 F g−1) and other reported MnO2-based electrodes listed in Table S2. Based on the specific capacitance plots, pristine ε-MnO2 exhibited capacitance retention of 64.4% in a current density of 1–10 A g−1. In contrast, the CQDs/ε-MnO2 delivers capacitance retention of 76.5% under the same condition, indicating better rate capability after the in-situ embedding of CQDs. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test was performed to investigate the charge transfer behavior of the pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes (Fig. 5f). The nearly vertical line in the low-frequency indicates the kinetics of the charge diffusion process within the active material of the electrode [55]. The intersection of the horizontal axis in high-frequency region represents equivalent series resistance (R
s ), and the diameter of the semicircle in the middle and high-frequency region corresponds to the charge-transfer resistance (R
ct ) at the active material/electrolyte interface [56]. As shown in Fig. 5f, the relatively higher R
s of CQDs/ε-MnO2 in comparison with pristine ε-MnO2, which may be stemmed from the dense structures caused by the in-situ embedding of CQDs leads to the increase of contact resistance at the interface between the electroactive material and the current collector. The R
ct of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 is lower than that of the pristine ε-MnO2, suggesting that in-situ embedding of CQDs have a positive effect on the rapid transmission of ions and electrons in ε-MnO2. Simultaneously, CQDs/ε-MnO2 displays a more upright line in low-frequency, implying in-situ embedding of CQDs facilitated ion diffusion behavior. The EIS result persuasively proves that the in-situ embedding of CQDs can effectively enhance the electrical conductivity and promote the ion diffusion of the CQDs/ε-MnO2.

The cycle stability of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode was investigated by repeated GCD at 5 A g−1 and shown in Fig. 5g. The CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode retained 90% capacitance retention after 6 000 cycles, and pristine ε-MnO2 only achieve 63% capacitance retention under the same test condition, indicating that the CQDs play a significant role in the cyclic stability of the CQDs/ε-MnO2. To validate the dual role of CQDs as structural stabilizer and inducer, the phase structure and morphology of the pristine ε-MnO2 CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode after 6 000 cycles were characterized by XRD, SEM and TEM, and shown in Fig. S6-S9, As shown in Fig. S6, the pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 converted to mixed phases of α-MnO2( JCPDS, PDF No 72–1982) and α-MnO2( JCPDS, PDF No 44–0992) after 6 000 cycles, and the proportion of α-MnO2 (JCPDS, PDF No. 44–0992) in CQDs/ε-MnO2 is higher than pristine ε-MnO2. It can be seen from the SEM images of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode after 6 000 cycles in Fig. S7, apparent cracks and falls off of active materials can be observed in the pristine ε-MnO2 film in Fig. S7a, ultrathin ε-MnO2 nanosheets form a loose structure in Fig. S7b-c. The original dense structure of CQDs/ε-MnO2 composed of ultrathin nanosheets was well maintained on the surface of carbon cloth (Fig. S7d-e), and the CQDs/ε-MnO2 ultrathin nanosheets was thinner and more compact than that of ε-MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S7f), suggesting excellent structural stability after the in-situ embedding of CQDs. The TEM images of pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode after 6 000 cycles were shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9, respectively, which were consistent with XRD and SEM results. The TEM image in Fig. S9a shows that the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode was more dense than pristine ε-MnO2 (Fig. S8a) after 6 000 cycles, and tiny CQDs are evenly embedded into the ε-MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S9b). The resolved lattice fringes with the spacings of 0.24 nm and 0.31 nm in HRTEM images of (Fig. S8b-c) of the labeled area in Fig. S8a can be assigned to the (311) plane of α-MnO2 (JCPDS#44–0992) and (310) plane of α-MnO2 (JCPDS#72–1982). The same results were observed in Fig. S9c-d, and the defined lattice spacings of 0.21 nm in Fig. S8c-d correspond to the (101) crystallographic planes of graphitic carbon. The above results indicate that pristine ε-MnO2 and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes have undergone phase transformation during the long-term cycling. The capacitive performance of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode was measured under the bending state to demonstrate the flexibility of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode. Fig. 5h displays the CV curves of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode tested in a bent and normal state. No significant change in the curve was observed, demonstrating excellent flexibility and capacitance preservation capability of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode.

Overall, the enhanced capacitive performance and superior structural stability of CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode indicate that a moderate amount of in-situ embedding of CQDs has significant positive effects on the as-prepared flexible electrode. First, CQDs can induce low-crystalline ε-MnO2, the presence of structural disorder can significantly increase efficient active sites and promote active site exposure for energy storage. In addition, the rich oxygen vacancies and the high Mn3+/Mn4+ ratio is feasible for enhancing the intrinsic electronic conductivity and the capacitive contribution of ε-MnO2. Then, the good anchor-hold between CQDs and ε-MnO2 not only can stabilize the original structure of ε-MnO2, but also facilitate the formation of strong interfacial bonding between ε-MnO2 and carbon cloth by the abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs, and finally obtain the robust electrodes with good mechanical properties.

To estimate the applicability of the CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrode in supercapacitor, an asymmetrical supercapacitor device (ASC) was assembled using CQDs/ε-MnO2 as positive electrode and ACC electrode (prepared in Section S1, supporting information) as negative electrode. Fig. S10 shows the capacitive performances of ACC. The two electrodes were separated with a separator soaked in the electrolyte of 1 M Na2SO4 to form a sandwich structure (Fig. S11a), and the ASC was marked as CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC in the following part. Fig. S11b displays the CV curves of ACC and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes in separate operation windows of − 1.0–0 V and 0–1.0 V at 10 mV s−1. The recorded potential windows and the matched capacitance of ACC and CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrodes enable the electrodes to assemble an ASC with a voltage window of 2.0 V. The CV curves of the CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC device within various voltage windows is shown in Fig. 6a, all the curves exhibit a nearly rectangular shape, and still retained the original shape at 2 V, suggesting the voltage windows of the CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASCs could be stable up to 2 V. Such a conclusion was further confirmed by the symmetrical GCD curves in Fig. 6b. Fig. 6c presents the CV curves of CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC at varied scan rates, the geometry of the CV curve has no evident change even at a high scan rate of 100 mV s−1, demonstrating the prominent capacitive characteristics and outstanding rate capability of CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC. The GCD curves of the assembled ASC in Fig. 6d exhibit symmetrical triangle shapes, indicating the excellent electrochemical reversibility and high coulombic efficiency. As shown in Fig. 6e, the assembled ASC achieved the specific capacitance of 124 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and remains 75 F g−1 at 10 A g−1. The energy (E) and power densities (P) of CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC were calculated according to the Eqs. (2) and (3) and plotted on the Ragone plots. As shown in Fig. 6f, the maximum energy density of CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC was 68.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 990 W kg−1, and still retained 41.25 Wh kg−1 with 9900 W kg−1. These results indicate that the CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC displays competitive and favorable energy and power densities compared with recently reported MnO2-based ASC (see Table S3). The CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC exhibited outstanding cycle stability with 96.5% retention of the initial capacitance and a coulombic efficiency of nearly 100% after 6 000 repeating GCD cycles at 5 A g−1 (Fig. 6g), indicating favorable durability and good cyclic reversibility. Moreover, the CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC was able to work normally even in a bending state. The digital photos in Fig. 6h-i show that an electronic compass can be powered by CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC ASC at normal and bending state, demonstrating its attractive flexibility and practicability.

In summary, CQDs/ε-MnO2 with stable structure and the desired morphology was successfully grown on the surface of carbon cloth through simple one-step electrodeposition. The results of XRD, Raman, XPS, EPR, and UV-DRS analysis have confirmed that CQDs/ε-MnO2 with abundant oxygen vacancies and low-crystalline were induced by in-situ embedding of the CQDs into the ε-MnO2. The electrochemical and mechanical tests showed that in-situ embedding of CQDs had significant positive effects on the electrochemical and mechanical performances of CQDs/ε-MnO2 flexible electrode. The CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode exhibited a high tensile strength of 87 MPa, larger than 48 MPa of pristine ε-MnO2. The obtained CQDs/ε-MnO2 electrode delivered a specific capacitance of 334.5 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with excellent rate capability with 76.5% capacitance retention at 10 A g−1 and cycling stability with 90% capacitance retention after 6 000 cycles at 5 A g−1. In addition, the assembled asymmetric CQDs/ε-MnO2//ACC devices delivered an energy density of 68.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 990 W kg−1. The work highlights the intriguing strategy toward robust structure and excellent capacitive performance enabled by in-situ embedding of CQDs, which can be adaptable to other electrode materials for flexible energy storage devices.

Hongying Quan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Weiliang Zeng: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wenchao Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Yan Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Wenhao Tao: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Dezhi Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 52063023) of China, the Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 20202ACBL204012) of Jiangxi Province, China.
